---
title: "Topological defects: flying through space glued to itself"
date: 2026-06-17
description: "A real-time 3D simulation of how a topological defect in space would look to an observer free to fly around — and through — it."
categories: [physics, topology, graphics]
image: topological-defects/thumb.png
resources:
  - topological-defects/js/
  - topological-defects/vendor/
  - topological-defects/viewer.html
  - topological-defects/scene.png
---

**A topological defect** is a region of space that has been cut out and
re-glued to itself. Its boundary faces are identified in pairs by rigid
motions, so light — and an observer — that leaves through one wall returns
through the partner wall, possibly rotated. The simplest case is a cube whose
opposite faces are glued straight across by translation: a perfectly
transparent block that simply displaces whatever you see through it. Twist the
gluing, or glue the faces of a different polyhedron, and you get a whole zoo of
richer defects — the *space forms* and *geons* that classify the closed
3-manifolds.

This project makes those abstract identifications something you can *see and
move through*. You take the seat of a free-floating camera in a stylized open
world — rolling hills, a winding river under a stone bridge, cottages, a ring
of mountains, drifting clouds — and nine defects float above the meadow, each
marked by a signpost. Fly through one and the full gluing is applied to you,
orientation included; the view then rights itself with a short, springy wobble.

## The mathematics

Each identified face carries a **gluing isometry** — a rotation $R$ and a
translation $\mathbf{t}$, applied in the defect's local frame as

$$ g(\mathbf{x}) = R\,\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{t}. $$

The familiar "twist about the face's own normal" family is the special case
$R = R(\hat{\mathbf n},\theta)$, $\mathbf{t} = -D\,\hat{\mathbf n}$; the more
exotic cells supply an arbitrary $(R,\mathbf{t})$ — and for the
non-orientable cells $R$ is a *reflection* ($\det R = -1$), which is exactly
what hands the world back mirror-reversed as you cross.

The signs are colour-coded by **orientability** — whether a consistent sense of
handedness survives every loop through the cell:

- **yellow** signs mark the **orientable** cells — here, all six flat platycosms;
- **green** signs mark the **non-orientable** ones, where crossing a
  glide-reflected wall hands the world back mirror-reversed, so "left" and
  "right" stop being globally defined.

Written into each yellow sign is a second property, **spinoriality** — whether
the cell's underlying 3-manifold admits half-integer spin, the Friedman–Sorkin
mechanism by which topology alone lets a region of space behave like a fermion.
The classification is clean: by Hendriks' theorem the only non-spinorial prime
3-manifolds are $S^3$, $S^2\times S^1$, and the lens spaces, so every flat
orientable cell here is spinorial and its sign reads **(spin $\tfrac12$)**; the
**(spin 1)** tag returns with the lens spaces. The non-orientable cells carry
pin± rather than spin structure, so they take no tag.

## Explore

::: {layout-ncol=1}

- **[Navigator →](topological-defects/index.html)** — the full open world. Take
  the observer's seat and fly through all nine defects in one continuous
  landscape. *(Desktop; mouse + keyboard.)*

- **[Gallery →](topological-defects/gallery.html)** — a grid of windows, one per
  manifold. Orbit each defect on its own and read its plaque. *(Works beautifully
  on a phone.)*

- **[Documentation →](topological-defects/docs.html)** — the mathematics of the
  defects, the story of how the visualization was built (wormholes and all), and
  the rendering technology behind it.

:::

## The defects

Nine gluing-cells, all of them closed, flat, and prime — the space forms you
get by gluing a Euclidean solid to itself.

- **Orientable** (the six *platycosms*) — the 3-torus $T^3$, the half-, third-,
  quarter- and sixth-turn flat space forms, and the Hantzsche–Wendt *didicosm*
  that completes the list.
- **Non-orientable** — three of the four non-orientable flat manifolds: the
  **first amphicosm** (Klein bottle $\times S^1$), the **second amphicosm** (its
  swap-glide sibling, glued across a diagonal mirror), and the **first
  amphidicosm**, the mirror counterpart of the Hantzsche–Wendt cell. Cross a
  glide-reflected wall in any of them and the world comes back left–right
  reversed; cross it twice and the reversal cancels.

The twisted cube cells (the quarter- and half-turn) are shown as *flattened
stand-ins* — all three face-pairs twist, so their edges carry conical seams
(deficit angles) the smooth manifold has not. That is the real physics of such a
defect, not a rendering artefact; the
[documentation](topological-defects/docs.html) explains where the honesty line
falls, why the curved space forms were pulled out pending a faithful rebuild,
and why four of the eight Thurston geometries can't be shown this way at all.
